turns.content: why does this even work this way? I'm trying to load a PNG file using the image crate but my code just crashes immediately. It seems like I have to manually handle memory allocation and then pass ownership around, which feels really clunky compared to how Python or C++ libraries seem to make it feel.

That frustration is common when you first switch from high-level languages to Rust because the image crate enforces strict ownership rules. When you call image::open() on a file path, it returns an OwnedImage which owns the raw buffer internally; you cannot just pass this around like a reference without cloning or converting it into something like BoxedImage or RgbImage first. The key insight is that Rust's safety comes from ensuring memory safety at compile time so you never get double-free errors when resizing or saving formats later, but it means you must explicitly choose between mutable and immutable variants depending on whether your pixel manipulation logic changes the original data.

I'm following a tutorial on the image crate and I'm stuck. Why does this even work this way? I just loaded a PNG file into an Image buffer, resized it with the resize function, and now I can't seem to get the pixel data out as a Vec<u8>. The documentation says you need to iterate over pixels but I don't understand why the API is structured like that instead of just giving me the raw bytes directly.

The reason the image crate exposes pixel data this way is because it separates high-level image manipulation from low-level byte access. When you load a PNG or JPEG, you get an Image object that tracks dimensions and format. The resize function returns a new Image with updated width and height. To actually read the pixel values, you must iterate over the pixels using the iterator trait on the Image struct. This design forces you to think about image geometry before accessing memory layout.
Ah okay so I need to call .pixels() and then loop through it? But wait, doesn't that mean I'm manually walking the internal array which feels inefficient compared to just dumping the buffer?

You are correct to question efficiency but there is a crucial distinction here. The Image struct does not expose its internal Vec<u8> directly because it can change internally during operations like resize or format conversion without breaking external references. If you were to access the raw buffer directly, any subsequent mutation would corrupt your data pointer. The pixel iterator yields (u32) tuples representing RGBA values in a specific order that matches how the image is stored in memory. This ensures safety and consistency.
